questions.question: Under s.2 of the Criminal Law Act 1997, what is the definition of an 'arrestable offence'?

options.A: Any offence for which a term of imprisonment of two years or more may be imposed
options.B: Any offence for which a term of imprisonment of five years or more may be imposed
options.C: Any offence that is triable on indictment before a jury
options.D: Any offence that is not a minor offence within the meaning of Art 38.2 of the Constitution

questions.explanation: Section 2 of the Criminal Law Act 1997 defines an 'arrestable offence' as any offence for which a person of full capacity and not previously convicted may, under or by virtue of any enactment, be punished by imprisonment for a term of five years or more, or by a more severe penalty. This replaced the old common law distinction between felonies and misdemeanours (abolished by s.3 CLA 1997). The five-year threshold is critical for determining arrest powers, bail considerations (Bail Act 1997 also uses five years as the 'serious offence' threshold), and other procedural consequences. Options A (two years) and C (indictable) are incorrect thresholds. Option D conflates the constitutional 'minor offence' category with the statutory 'arrestable offence' definition.

questions.question: The Criminal Law Act 1997, s.3, abolished the distinction between felonies and misdemeanours in Irish law. Before abolition, what practical significance did this distinction have?

options.A: Felonies were tried by a judge alone; misdemeanours required a jury
options.B: Felonies attracted the power of arrest without warrant at common law; misdemeanours generally did not
options.C: Felonies carried mandatory life sentences; misdemeanours could only be punished by fines
options.D: Felonies were tried in the High Court; misdemeanours were tried in the Circuit Court

questions.explanation: At common law, one of the key practical distinctions between felonies and misdemeanours was the power of arrest: a constable (and sometimes a private citizen) could arrest without warrant for a felony committed or reasonably suspected. Misdemeanours generally did not attract warrantless arrest at common law. Other distinctions included: the right to legal representation (historically restricted for felonies), the treatment of accomplices, and certain evidential rules. The 1997 Act replaced this with the concept of 'arrestable offences' (any offence carrying five years or more) as the basis for arrest powers, making the practical effect more rational and clear. Options A, C, and D incorrectly state features of the old distinction — felony/misdemeanour was not based on jury trial mode, mandatory sentences, or specific courts.

questions.question: Maria is charged with assault causing harm under s.3 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997. The offence is triable either way. The DPP consents to summary trial. What additional condition must be satisfied before the District Court can deal with the matter summarily?

options.A: The accused must consent to summary trial
options.B: The District Court must be satisfied that the offence is minor in nature and appropriate for summary disposal
options.C: The victim must provide a statement agreeing to summary proceedings
options.D: A senior Garda officer must certify that the offence does not warrant indictment

questions.explanation: For a hybrid (triable either way) offence to be dealt with summarily in the District Court, two conditions must be met: (1) the DPP must consent to summary trial; and (2) the District Court must be satisfied that the offence is minor in nature within the meaning of Art 38.2 of the Constitution and appropriate for summary disposal (s.2 Criminal Justice Act 1951). The accused's consent is not one of the formal conditions, though the accused may have a right to elect jury trial in some circumstances. The victim's views and Garda certification are not part of the legal test. This two-part test balances the DPP's prosecution discretion with the court's constitutional obligation to ensure that non-minor offences are not improperly disposed of summarily.

questions.question: Article 38.5 of the Irish Constitution provides for the right to trial by jury. Which of the following is NOT an exception to this right recognised by the Constitution?

options.A: Minor offences triable in courts of summary jurisdiction (Art 38.2)
options.B: Offences tried before the Special Criminal Court (Art 38.3)
options.D: Offences where the accused is legally represented and waives their jury trial right

questions.explanation: Article 38.5 provides that no person shall be tried on any criminal charge without a jury, subject to three exceptions expressly stated in the Constitution: (1) Art 38.2 — minor offences may be tried in courts of summary jurisdiction without a jury; (2) Art 38.3 — offences may be tried by special courts where ordinary courts are inadequate (i.e., the Special Criminal Court); (3) Art 38.4 — offences tried by military tribunals. There is no constitutional provision allowing jury trial to be waived by an accused in Ireland, unlike some other jurisdictions. The accused cannot simply choose a bench trial. Option D is therefore NOT a recognised constitutional exception. This is a critical distinction from jurisdictions like the US or UK where waiver of jury trial is possible.

questions.question: The Central Criminal Court has exclusive jurisdiction over certain serious indictable offences. Which of the following offences must be tried in the Central Criminal Court?

options.A: Aggravated burglary under s.13 of the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001
options.B: Causing serious harm under s.4 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997
options.C: Murder, rape, treason, piracy, and aggravated sexual assault
options.D: Any offence carrying a maximum penalty of life imprisonment

questions.explanation: Under s.29 of the Criminal Procedure Act 1967, the Central Criminal Court (which is the High Court sitting in its criminal jurisdiction, per s.2 Courts (Establishment and Constitution) Act 1961) has exclusive jurisdiction over: murder, attempted murder, conspiracy to murder, rape, aggravated sexual assault, rape under s.4, and treason. These are the most serious offences in Irish law. Option A (aggravated burglary) and Option B (causing serious harm) are serious indictable offences triable in the Circuit Court, not exclusively the Central Criminal Court. Option D is incorrect — life imprisonment is not the criterion; aggravated burglary carries life but is tried in the Circuit Court.

questions.question: In Melling v Mathghamhna [1962] IR 1 and O'Sullivan v Hartnett, the courts considered the constitutional meaning of 'minor offence' for the purposes of Art 38.2. What is the correct test for determining whether an offence is 'minor'?

options.A: An offence is minor if it is described as 'minor' or 'summary' in the relevant statute
options.B: An offence is minor if the maximum penalty is less than 12 months imprisonment or a fine below a certain threshold, assessed by reference to the gravity of the offence and the severity of the punishment
options.C: An offence is minor if the DPP elects to proceed summarily
options.D: An offence is minor if it does not involve physical violence

questions.explanation: The constitutional determination of whether an offence is 'minor' within Art 38.2 is not based solely on the statutory label. Irish courts assess the gravity of the offence and the potential punishment: an offence is minor if its penalties are sufficiently limited so that it would not be unconstitutional to try it without a jury. Penalties of up to 12 months imprisonment or moderate fines are generally considered consistent with 'minor' classification. The courts (in cases like Melling, O'Sullivan v Hartnett, and Cartmill v Ireland) have recognised that the legislature has some discretion in classifying offences, but this classification is reviewable if the penalty is so severe as to require jury trial under Art 38.5. Option A — statutory label — is relevant but not conclusive. Option C — DPP election — is procedurally relevant for hybrid offences but does not define constitutional 'minority.' Option D — absence of violence — is not the test.

questions.question: A client asks their solicitor to explain the difference between a 'summary offence' and a 'hybrid offence.' Which of the following is the most accurate explanation?

options.A: A summary offence can only ever be tried in the District Court without a jury; a hybrid offence can be tried either in the District Court without a jury or on indictment before a jury, subject to the DPP's consent and the court's satisfaction that it is minor
options.B: A summary offence is always less serious than a hybrid offence, which is always less serious than an indictable offence
options.C: A summary offence is tried by a judge alone; a hybrid offence is tried by a judge and two lay assessors
options.D: Both summary and hybrid offences are tried in the District Court, but hybrid offences require a jury of six persons

questions.explanation: A summary offence is one that can only be tried in the District Court without a jury (Art 38.2 Constitution; s.5(1) Courts Act 1961). A hybrid or 'triable either way' offence is one that the legislature has made capable of trial in either the District Court (without jury) or on indictment before a jury in the Circuit Court, depending on: (1) DPP consent to summary trial, and (2) the District Court being satisfied the offence is minor in nature (s.2 Criminal Justice Act 1951). Option B is factually inaccurate — penalty/gravity determines seriousness, not classification per se. Option C is incorrect — there are no 'lay assessors' in Irish criminal law. Option D is incorrect — District Court trials never involve a jury in any form.

questions.question: The Special Criminal Court was established under which constitutional provision, and what condition justifies its use?

options.A: Art 38.2 — where the offence is minor and does not warrant jury trial
options.B: Art 38.3 — where the ordinary courts are inadequate to secure the effective administration of justice, and the preservation of public peace and order
options.C: Art 38.5 — where both parties to the proceedings consent to a non-jury trial
options.D: Art 40.4 — where the accused poses a flight risk that cannot be addressed through bail conditions

questions.explanation: Article 38.3.1 of the Constitution provides that special courts may be established by law for the trial of offences in cases where it may be determined in accordance with such law that the ordinary courts are inadequate to secure the effective administration of justice and the preservation of public peace and order. The Special Criminal Court sits with three judges and no jury (Part V OATSA 1939). In Kavanagh v Ireland [1996] IESC, the Supreme Court upheld the constitutionality of the SCC and the DPP's wide discretion to direct cases there without giving reasons. Option A is wrong — Art 38.2 covers minor offences in summary courts, not the SCC. Option C is wrong — Art 38.5 is the general jury right provision, not the SCC's basis, and consent cannot waive jury trial in Ireland. Option D — Art 40.4 — concerns habeas corpus and detention, not the SCC.

questions.question: A client is charged with theft under s.4 of the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001. The solicitor knows theft carries a maximum of 10 years on indictment. Theft is a hybrid offence. The DPP has consented to summary trial. The District Court states it is not satisfied the offence is minor in nature. What happens?

options.A: The District Court must accept summary jurisdiction once the DPP consents
options.B: The District Court must proceed to hear the case summarily but may impose a reduced penalty
options.C: The case must be sent forward for trial on indictment to the Circuit Court, as both conditions for summary trial (DPP consent and District Court satisfaction) must be met
options.D: The client can override the District Court's view by electing to be tried summarily

questions.explanation: For a hybrid offence to be tried summarily, BOTH conditions must be satisfied: (1) DPP consent, AND (2) the District Court must be satisfied that the offence is minor in nature (s.2 CJA 1951). If the District Court is not satisfied that the offence is minor — perhaps because of the value involved, the circumstances, or the accused's record — it cannot deal with the matter summarily regardless of DPP consent. In such circumstances the case must be sent forward for trial on indictment in the Circuit Court. Option A incorrectly suggests DPP consent alone is sufficient. Option B is wrong — if the District Court declines summary jurisdiction it cannot impose a reduced penalty. Option D is wrong — the accused's election is relevant in some contexts but cannot override the District Court's constitutional obligation not to try non-minor offences without a jury.
